verses.text: And it came to pass, that after the year was expired, at the time that kings go out to battle, Joab led forth the power of the army, and wasted the country of the children of Ammon, and came and besieged Rabbah. But David tarried at Jerusalem. And Joab smote Rabbah, and destroyed it. And David took the crown of their king from off his head, and found it to weigh a talent of gold, and there were precious stones in it; and it was set upon David's head: and he brought also exceeding much spoil out of the city. And he brought out the people that were in it, and cut them with saws, and with harrows of iron, and with axes. Even so dealt David with all the cities of the children of Ammon. And David and all the people returned to Jerusalem. Guidance: The Ammonite war is brought to its end: Joab besieges and destroys Rabbah, and David takes the enemy king's crown — a talent of gold set with precious stones — upon his own head, with great spoil. Yet the Chronicler drops a quiet, telling phrase into the account: 'But David tarried at Jerusalem.' At 'the time that kings go out to battle,' David stayed behind at ease, and the watchful reader of Scripture knows that it was precisely in this season of idleness at home that David fell into his great sin (2 Sam 11). The chapter is showing us, in that brief 'but David tarried,' a sober warning hidden within a triumph. The chapter is teaching us the danger of being out of our proper place and calling; when David should have been with his army, he lingered in ease, and idleness opened the door to temptation. We are most vulnerable when we are not where duty calls us. The chapter is teaching us that even a man after God's own heart could stumble when he stepped aside from his post — and that no past victory or present prosperity makes us safe from sin. The chapter is teaching us to keep diligently to the work God has given us, for an idle and self-indulgent ease is the soil in which great falls take root. Yet the chapter is also teaching us, in the Chronicler's gracious silence about the sin itself, the mercy of God who covers the iniquity of the penitent; here David is shown crowned and victorious, his sin forgiven and not paraded, a picture of how God deals with His restored servant. The crown of gold upon David's head reminds us that the LORD exalted His anointed in spite of his failings — and points us to great David's Son, the King who never tarried from His appointed work, who finished all He was given to do, and who wears a crown that no sin can tarnish.

And it came to pass after this, that there arose war at Gezer with the Philistines; at which time Sibbechai the Hushathite slew Sippai, that was of the children of the giant: and they were subdued. And there was war again with the Philistines; and Elhanan the son of Jair slew Lahmi the brother of Goliath the Gittite, whose spear staff was like a weaver's beam. And yet again there was war at Gath, where was a man of great stature, whose fingers and toes were four and twenty, six on each hand, and six on each foot: and he also was the son of the giant. But when he defied Israel, Jonathan the son of Shimea David's brother slew him. These were born unto the giant in Gath; and they fell by the hand of David, and by the hand of his servants. Guidance: The chapter closes with a series of victories over the giants of Gath — the kindred of Goliath, including 'Lahmi the brother of Goliath... whose spear staff was like a weaver's beam,' and a monstrous man with six fingers on each hand and six toes on each foot. One by one these giants who 'defied Israel' are struck down, not now by David alone (as Goliath had been in his youth) but by David's servants — Sibbechai, Elhanan, and Jonathan David's nephew. 'They fell by the hand of David, and by the hand of his servants.' The chapter is showing us that the giants who once filled Israel with terror are now routinely overcome; what once required a special deliverance through the young David's faith is now the ordinary work of his trained and faithful men. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD gives His people increasing victory over the enemies that once seemed invincible; the seed of the giant, the very brothers of Goliath, all fall before the servants of God's king. The chapter is teaching us a great encouragement: the giants do fall. The defiant powers that loom so large and boast their spears like weavers' beams are no match for the people of God who go against them in faith. The chapter is teaching us, too, that the victory of the captain becomes the victory of his followers; David had conquered Goliath, and now his servants conquer Goliath's whole house. So the triumph of our Captain, Christ, who has overcome the great enemy, becomes the triumph of His servants, who in His strength tread down the giants that defy them. The chapter is teaching us not to fear the sons of the giant; they fell by the hand of David and his servants, and every defiant foe of God's people shall likewise fall. What our King began, His servants are enabled to finish — for the giants are mortal, and the LORD gives the victory.
